1: Ps 113: Opening of the Egyptian Hallel — Universal Praise, Particular Reversal. Pss 113-118 form the 'Hallel' (Egyptian Hallel or Hallel HaMitzri), sung at the three pilgrimage festivals (Passover, Weeks, Tabernacles). At Passover, Pss 113-114 were sung before the meal and Pss 115-118 after it (the 'hymn' Jesus and the disciples sang in Matt 26:30; Mark 14:26 was likely Pss 115-118). 'Praise the LORD! Praise, O servants of the LORD, praise the name of the LORD!' — the triple imperative of praise (hallelu Yah halelu avdei YHWH halelu et shem YHWH) — the servants of YHWH, his worshiping community, are summoned to praise the divine Name. The Name is what is praised — the character and attributes of YHWH as revealed in covenant history.
2: 'Blessed be the name of the LORD from this time forth and forevermore!' — the blessing of the divine Name: yehi shem YHWH mevorach (let the name of the LORD be blessed). 'From this time forth and forevermore' (me'atah vead olam) — temporal universality matching the geographic universality of v3. The blessing spans all time and all space. This verse and v3 together establish the cosmic scope of the worship that the particular community in Jerusalem was conducting.
3: 'From the rising of the sun to its setting, the name of the LORD is to be praised!' — 'from the rising of the sun to its setting' (mimmizrach shemesh ad mevo'o) — east to west: the full geographic sweep of the inhabited world. The prayer that YHWH's name would be praised across all geography is the Psalter's universalism in miniature. Mal 1:11 uses identical language: 'from the rising of the sun to its setting my name will be great among the nations' — the eschatological vision that the Hallel anticipates.
4: 'The LORD is high above all nations, and his glory above the heavens!' — 'high above all nations' (ram al kol goyim YHWH) — political universalism: YHWH's authority exceeds that of every national patron deity. 'His glory above the heavens' (veal hashamayim kevodo) — cosmological universalism: even the heavens, the highest reach of created space, do not contain or define YHWH's glory. The Hallel's praise is not parochial; it addresses the transcendent God of all creation.
5: 'Who is like the LORD our God, who is seated on high' — the incomparability question (mi kamohw YHWH Eloheinu) — the rhetorical question expecting no answer. 'Seated on high' (hammagbihi lashevet) — the divine throne elevated beyond all created thrones. The incomparability formula appears throughout OT (Exod 15:11; Pss 35:10; 89:6; Isa 40:18,25) as the foundational theological affirmation: no other being compares to YHWH.
6: '...who looks far down on the heavens and the earth?' — 'looks far down on the heavens and the earth' (hamashpili lir'ot bashamayim uvaaretz) — the paradox: YHWH must 'stoop down' (shaphal, to humble oneself, to descend) even to look at the heavens. The highest point of creation is below YHWH's vantage. This divine transcendence is not remoteness but the precondition of the radical condescension that follows in vv7-9.
7: 'He raises the poor from the dust and lifts the needy from the ash heap' — the pivot to particular action. The transcendent God of vv4-6 acts specifically for the lowest human beings. 'Raises the poor from the dust' (meqimi meaphar dal) and 'lifts the needy from the ash heap' (meashpot yarim evyon) — this verse is borrowed almost verbatim from Hannah's Song (1 Sam 2:8: 'He raises up the poor from the dust; he lifts the needy from the ash heap'). Hannah's prayer at the birth of Samuel, and the Psalm's echo of it, create a trajectory: the barren woman given a son becomes the type for all divine reversals.
8: '...to make them sit with princes, with the princes of his people' — 'to make them sit with princes' (lehoshivi im nedivim) — the social reversal: the ash-heap poor seated with the nobles. 'With the princes of his people' (im nedivei amo) — not foreign nobility but Israel's own leadership: the covenant community's highest seats are given to those the world would place lowest. Mary's Magnificat (Luke 1:52: 'he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate') draws on this text through Hannah's song.
9: 'He gives the barren woman a home, making her the joyful mother of children. Praise the LORD!' — the third reversal: barrenness to motherhood. The barren woman (aqeret habbayit, the childless woman of the house) is given a home and made a 'joyful mother of children' (em habanim semecha). The barren-to-fruitful theme runs through the matriarchal narratives: Sarah (Gen 21), Rebekah (Gen 25:21), Rachel (Gen 30:22), and Hannah (1 Sam 1-2). The Hallel psalm closes with the same reversal that Hannah's prayer celebrated — the God who grants children to the barren is the God who raised up the poor from the dust. The 'Hallelujah!' closes the psalm as it opened it.
